transcript.speaker: Varsha
transcript.text: Rakesh — first thing. Redis is still not up. I've been writing the async queue code against a mock but I can't do proper integration testing without real infra. What's the status Vivek?

transcript.speaker: Vivek
transcript.text: Vinod sir's mail went yesterday afternoon. Infra team replied this morning — they said they'll provision Redis by 2pm today. I've been following up since 8am.

transcript.speaker: Rakesh
transcript.text: Okay. Varsha — keep going with mock till 2pm. What percentage of the code can you complete without real infra?

transcript.speaker: Varsha
transcript.text: Producer side is fully done. Consumer logic is about 70% done. The retry scheduler and dead-letter queue handling — those I need to test against real Redis. So maybe 60-65% overall without infra.

transcript.speaker: Rakesh
transcript.text: That's fine. You have today afternoon and tomorrow. Ajay how are UAT preparations going?

transcript.speaker: Ajay
transcript.text: I'm preparing the UAT scenario document for client's team. But I've been thinking about what client testers will actually try. Not just happy path. Things like — user closes the browser in the middle of a payment. Same order getting submitted twice if someone clicks pay twice. Refund initiated immediately after payment before it's fully settled. These are business edge cases, not just technical.

transcript.speaker: Rupali
transcript.text: Those are exactly the kind of things client UAT team will do on Day 1.

transcript.speaker: Rakesh
transcript.text: Ajay good thinking. Varsha, Suraj — I need both of you to sit with Ajay for an hour today. Walk her through your code paths so she understands what's handled and what isn't. Then we can plug the gaps before Monday.

transcript.speaker: Varsha
transcript.text: After 2pm works, once Redis is hopefully up and I've switched from mock.

transcript.speaker: Suraj
transcript.text: 3pm for me. I have a design review till 2:30.

transcript.speaker: Ajay
transcript.text: 3pm works. I'll prepare the scenario list and we go through it together.

transcript.speaker: Rakesh
transcript.text: Good. Rupali — you mentioned the intermittent prod failures are still open. Varsha, any new hits?

transcript.speaker: Varsha
transcript.text: Yes. Two more occurred yesterday evening, between 7 and 9pm. That's consistent — peak usage time. The async queue will directly solve this. Instead of the request failing, it'll queue and retry automatically. But right now those 2 cases are still live in prod.

transcript.speaker: Ajay
transcript.text: So going into UAT Monday we potentially still have live intermittent failures in prod. If client's UAT team hits one of those during testing —

transcript.speaker: Rakesh
transcript.text: That's exactly why we need the async queue done before Monday. Varsha — realistic check. If Redis is up at 2pm today, can you be done by tomorrow EOD?

transcript.speaker: Varsha
transcript.text: If nothing unexpected comes up — yes. Tomorrow EOD is achievable.

transcript.speaker: Rakesh
transcript.text: Then Ajay needs Saturday to test the async queue. That leaves Sunday as pure buffer if anything breaks.

transcript.speaker: Vinod
transcript.text: Sunday buffer is not comfortable. What if something comes up Sunday?

transcript.speaker: Rakesh
transcript.text: Sir I understand. But honestly — we lost 2 days in week 1 to infra environment issues. Day 1 the env wasn't set up. Day 4 the SSL cert. Those weren't development delays. We're working with the timeline we have left.

transcript.speaker: Vinod
transcript.text: Okay. I hear you. Let's make it work. Everyone on standby this weekend.

transcript.speaker: Vivek
transcript.text: I'll be available. Moment Redis is up I'll ping Varsha directly so she doesn't have to wait.
